coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent detection of Pb2+ pollutant in water samples with the help of Delonix regia leaf-derived CQDs
coredata.dc:creator: Babu, Ann Mariella
Bijoy, Geethanjali
Keerthana, P.
Varghese, Anitha
coredata.dc:description: Heavy metals released from different sources into water bodies are a major concern in the view of environmental protection. Their non-biodegradability and the numerous health hazards add to the issue. Scientists worldwide have emphasized the issue and are trying to resolve it by different means. Among all the methods, the fluorescent method stands out for its simplicity and rapid results. Here, the study focuses on the development of an efficient and sustainable method for the detection of lead in waste-water effluents. Carbon quantum dots (GCDs), a highly non-toxic substance developed from Delonix regia leaves for the purpose via a simple hydrothermal method. The prepared GCDs exhibited good photostability and water solubility; they also displayed a bright red emission when irradiated with ultraviolet (UV) light. Here, the authors propose the synthesized GCDs as an effective fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of lead ions in aqueous samples. Photoluminescence quenching of GCDs by the addition of lead was studied and a good detection limit of 3.3 nM with a linear range of 10–180 µM was achieved. Further, real sample analysis was also conducted to prove the applicability of the synthesized GCDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Delonix regia leaves
Hydrothermal synthesis
Carbon quantum dots
Pb2+ ions
FRET

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of Pb2+ in water samples
PL response of GCDs on the addition of various metal ions and fluorescence stability investigations
Determination of PL stability of GCD and analysis of Pb2+ ion
Sensitivity determination of GCDs for the selective detection of Pb2+
Analysis of Pb2+ ion in waste-water samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The researchers and authors express their gratitude to the Major research project (MRP DSC-1830 ), Center for Research, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bangalore, Karnataka, India for providing the required financial support for completing this research work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a)Schematic Representation for the synthesis of GCD. (b)Mechanism of florescence quenching.
(a)TEM representation for GCD. (b) Histogram analysis for the particle size distribution of GCD. (c) XRD plot for GCD. (d) Raman spectrum of GCD.
XPS spectra of (a) Survey spectra. (b) C1s spectrum. (c) N1s Spectrum. (d) O1s spectrum.
(a) Absorption spectra for GCD in the presence and absence of lead ions. (b) Excitation and emission spectra for GCD.

(a) Excitation-dependent emission spectrum. (b) Area-normalized excitation-dependent emission spectrum. (c) Emission-dependent excitation spectrum. (d) contour mapping of the prepared GCD. (e) Zeta potential value of GCD.
GCDs stability studies (a) different time periods (b)number of days (c)different pH values and (d) ionic strength of KCl.

(a) Interference test of GCD solution for Pb2+ ions and other metal ions Mn 2+. (b) Varying concentration of lead ions up to 180 µM. (c) Graphical linear plot between the concentration of and PL intensity. (d) PL decay plot in the presence and absence of lead (Pb2+).
Comparison of the proposed GCD fluorescent sensor with other existing works for Pb2+ detection.

row.entry: dual- and three-emission carbon dot (CD) nanohybrids

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analysis of lead content in various waste-water effluents.
Analysis of lead content in industrial effluents.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent detection of Pb2+ pollutant in water samples with the help of Delonix regia leaf-derived CQDs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bangalore, Karnataka 560029, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, CHRIST (Deemed to be University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bangalore, Karnataka 560029, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heavy metals released from different sources into water bodies are a major concern in the view of environmental protection. Their non-biodegradability and the numerous health hazards add to the issue. Scientists worldwide have emphasized the issue and are trying to resolve it by different means. Among all the methods, the fluorescent method stands out for its simplicity and rapid results. Here, the study focuses on the development of an efficient and sustainable method for the detection of lead in waste-water effluents. Carbon quantum dots (GCDs), a highly non-toxic substance developed from Delonix regia leaves for the purpose via a simple hydrothermal method. The prepared GCDs exhibited good photostability and water solubility; they also displayed a bright red emission when irradiated with ultraviolet (UV) light. Here, the authors propose the synthesized GCDs as an effective fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of lead ions in aqueous samples. Photoluminescence quenching of GCDs by the addition of lead was studied and a good detection limit of 3.3 nM with a linear range of 10–180 µM was achieved. Further, real sample analysis was also conducted to prove the applicability of the synthesized GCDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals are naturally existing in the earth’s crust and are important components for maintaining marine and terrestrial habitats. But recently, the increasingly persistent accumulation of these heavy metals is alarming, owing to their stability and non-biodegradability [1]. Lead is recognized as one of the major heavy metal poisons by the World Health Organization. It is classified as a toxic environmental pollutant whose consumption can cause cancer, and kidney/brain-related dysfunction, in adults as well as children [2]. Mining, smelting, production of batteries, pigments, paints, plastics, automobile industries, fertilizers, etc. are the primary sources of lead release in the water bodies [3]. Studies have reported low memory power for children exposed to even low amounts of lead and often exhibit conduct problems [4]. WHO is constantly trying to make the regulatory standard for lead intake more stringent and has come up to 10 µg/L and this limit is provisional. Lately, developing countries are trying to phase out the use of lead gasoline and reduce its applications after realizing its dangerously high concentrations in drinking water samples [5–7]. The Bureau of Indian standard has allowed a 0.01 mg/L of lead for safe drinking water and any concentration above this limit is likely to cause public health concerns in the state. It is to be noted that lead does not enter the drinking water system through the raw water sources or as it leaves the water treatment plants. The major contribution of lead in drinking water comes from the facets and household plumbing systems across the country [2,8]. Further, in addition to the untreated industrial effluents [9], a large contribution of lead into the environmental water comes from different industries such as dyes and pigments, leather, and fertilizing industries which are significant sources [10]. They provide a way for heavy metals like lead, to mix in water bodies and enter our drinking water tanks [11–14]. Therefore, it is pertinent that we identify a rapid and accurate analytical method for precisely sensing and quantifying lead concentration in drinking water samples.

Among the several existing methods which are generally expensive and require expertise (such as chromatography, Raman spectroscopy, neutron activation analysis, atomic absorption spectroscopy, mass spectrometry, etc.) fluorescence probe methods have been identified as rapid, selective, sensitive, and offer easy operation [15–17]. One major limitation is their toxicity risks, non-biodegradable nature, as well as poor solubility of most of the organic fluorescent probes such as rhodamine derivatives, pyrene derivatives, carbon nanomaterials, etc.,[16,18].

To overcome these challenges, scientists have recently introduced a non-toxic nano-element, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a fluorescent sensing probe material. CQDs were obtained as a result of the purification of carbon nanotubes, and since then these materials have gathered huge interest community-wide, owing to their extraordinary physical properties, especially the luminescent property [19,20]. They are heavily exploited in applications such as bio-imaging, energy harvesting, biomedicines, photocatalysis, etc. [21–23]. Compared to the inorganic semiconductor quantum dots and these nanomaterials, CQDs offer tunable fluorescence emission, are capable of upgrading their selectivity according to the analyte, and also give a high quantum yield [24,25]. They can even be easily commercialized because of already existing and established synthesis procedures, the most common one being hydrothermal synthesis. The hydrothermal method is selected in this research work as the authors desire to obtain properly shaped quantum dots. The popularity of the method is due to its economic, environment-friendly nature, which requires only a simple setup and a highly pure, uniform product is obtained [15,20,25,26]. Therefore, here we have made use of the available data on the feasibility of CQDs as sensing materials and propose a novel fluorescent sensor derived from Delonix regia leaves, a commonly available flowering plant across India.

Here, biomass-derived carbon quantum dots are prepared since they are non-toxic and environment-friendly compared to chemically synthesized quantum dots. The obtained GCDs (Delonix regia derived carbon quantum dots) were synthesized from the hydrothermal method and it exhibits a size distribution between 6.75 ± 2.5 nm. The developed sensor is used for detecting heavy metal lead (Pb2+) in water samples via rapid quenching of GCDs fluorescence and gave a detection limit of 3.3 nM.

Delonix regia leaves were procured from Bangalore, Karnataka, and were used after proper washing and drying. Ethanol, KOH, buffer solution (KH2PO4 and K2HPO4), Rhodamine B, Metal salts of Pb2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Sn2+, Hg2+, Cr2+, and Fe2+. The chemicals obtained were all of the pure analytical grades and double distilled water was used for the preparation of all solutions.

Leaves from the Delonix regia plant were procured and washed several times thoroughly with distilled water to make them dust and impurity-free. The leaves were then transferred to the Soxhlet apparatus with ethanol solution for complete extraction. Later, the hence-obtained solution was poured into Teflon lined autoclave for hydrothermal treatment. The temperature inside the muffle furnace was set to 150 °C for six hours in order to prepare GCDs. There was a very evident color change of the solution as it turned from green to brown after the synthesis was complete, as shown in Fig. 1a. Once the reaction was completed, the brown color synthesized GCD underwent filtration through column chromatography and was further, filtered using the Whatman filter paper. The obtained brown solution was then subjected to centrifugation at a high speed of 30,000 rpm for 20 min. The obtained solution was dialyzed through a dialysis membrane for 48 h and further preserved in the refrigerator at a temperature of 4 °C for future studies.

In order to perform characterization analysis, FTIR (Fourier transform infrared), TEM, powder XRD (x-ray diffraction), Raman spectroscopy, XPS (X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy), and PL spectrofluorometer was employed. The FT-IR spectrum helped in learning about the functional groups on the surface of the prepared GCD sample. TEM images were recorded using the Thermo Nicolet, Avatar 370. JEOL (JEM-F200) electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV was employed. For the powder XRD analysis of the GCDs, the Rigaku SmartLab X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Ǻ) was accustomed. Further, to record the Raman spectrum, the Lab RAM HR FT-Raman module at 532 nm LASER was used. XPS spectrum was obtained using the ESCA- 14 (Omicron Nanotechnology) X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Moreover, the absorption measurements were performed on a Shimadzu UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV–visible 1800), and all the fluorescence measurements (excitation, emission, contour map, Photoluminescence (PL)) were conducted with the help of a Shimadzu spectrofluorometer (RF5301PC). Here, all the measurements were obtained by using PBS at pH 7 medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Pb2+ in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared GCDs were used to carry out PL (photoluminescence) quenching studies at room temperature to detect the presence of lead (Pb2+) in water samples. Solutions with varying concentrations of Pb2+ (ranging from 10 to 180 µl) were taken in the cuvette for investigations. To this 20 µl of GCD was added. Ethanol was added to dilute the solution. The PL of GCDs excitation and emission intensities were observed at 661 and 676 nm respectively. The result was used for the construction of a calibration plot to carry out the analysis of lead in water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL response of GCDs on the addition of various metal ions and fluorescence stability investigations
ce:section.ce:para: The interference studies were done to understand the selectivity of the hence-developed GCDs towards Pb2+. Here, aqueous solutions of several metal ions (such as Pb2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Sn2+, Hg2+, Cr2+, and Fe2+) were analyzed. 10 µl of GCD, 10 µl of metal ion, and ethanol were taken into the cuvette for PL study. The PL spectra of GCD for the presence and absence of each of these metal ions were recorded separately at an excitation wavelength of 661 nm. The examination for fluorescence stability was conducted with respect to different pH values, ionic strengths, UV irradiation, and long storage duration. An excitation wavelength of 661 nm was directed to obtain the PL spectra for the GCDs from a spectrophotometer.

The prepared GCDs were employed for lead sensing in real water-effluent samples collected from sources close to the industrial areas (river water, lake water, and tap water). Here, various concentrations of lead were added to the water samples after their filtration with Whatman paper, and analysis was done. The spiked water samples were checked for the presence of Pb2+ ions. The experiment was conducted at room temperature.

Here, the PL-quantum yield was calculated for GCDs using rhodamine as the reference. The absorbance was maintained below 0.1 in order to reduce the effects of re-absorption. The following formula was used for calculating the quantum yield of the GCDs.
∅GCD = ∅Rhodamine × (IGCD/IRhodamine) × (ŋ2 GCD/ŋ2 Rhodamine) × (ARhodamine/AGCD)
Where ∅GCD is the GCD quantum yield, ∅Rhodamine represents the reference quantum yield, IGCD and IRhodamine are the integrated emission intensities of the sample, and reference respectively, ŋ is the refractive index and A, the absorbance at 410 nm.

The GCDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal approach as explained in the experimental part. TEM analysis was carried out to study the shape and size of the GCD particle. The quantum dots were revealed to have a quasi-spherical morphology as desired (shown in Fig. 2a). The results indicated an average diameter of the GCD to be in a narrow size distribution range of 6.75 ± 2.5 nm (Fig. 2b. The mono-dispersibility of the developed GCDs is confirmed through the obtained TEM results.

The synthesized GCDs’ XRD result patterns are displayed in Fig. 2c. A broad, single peak at around 2θ = 20.02 and a small hump that appeared at 40.1 were obtained from the XRD pattern. It also suggested an inter-layer distance of 0.43 nm. The peak is a confirmation of the amorphous nature of the carbon quantum dots with the sp3 domain. Bragg’s equation (given below) was employed in order to calculate the inter-layer distance,
d = nλ/2sinθ
Here, the inter-planar spacing is represented by d, θ gives the peak’s position, n is the diffraction order, and λ is the incident ray wavelength.

The GCDs Raman spectrum is shown in Fig. 2d. The two peaks representing the D & G bands, are positioned at 1343 and 1555 cm−1. The display of the D band (which has A1 g symmetry) represents the vibration of the C atom which is in the termination plane. Further, the G band (E2 g symmetry) represents the peak with respect to the C-C (bonded atoms) bond’s vibration present in the 2-D lattice. Both D and G bands are indicative of the extent of defects and the presence of disordered carbon [15]. The ratio of ID/IG obtained was about 1.91, hence substantiating that the GCDs prepared are amorphous in nature. The ID/IG value achieved here is the characteristic of the sp3/sp2 carbon domain.

For more sensitive information such as minute structural details, surface functionalization, electronic interactions, hydrogen bonding, etc. FT-IR instrument was employed. A typical FT-IR spectrum achieved for GCDs is given in Fig. 3. The FT-IR spectra recorded for the GCD showed an absorption band at 3371 cm−1 which corresponds to OH and NH stretching vibration. However, bands at 1173 cm−1 reveal the presence of amino groups like the C-N. Further, CH3 and CH2 groups are also confirmed by the stretching vibration bands obtained at 2926 cm−1 and 2854 cm−1. The weak bands at 1455 cm−1 and 1378 cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of C-H bonds respectively. At 1624 cm−1, a sharp band was noted which substantiates the existence of carbonyl (CO) vibration stretching. Also, the bands obtained at 1067 cm−1, and 1316 cm−1 correspond to the C-O and COO- bands respectively. The 1513 cm−1 weak band merged with the carbonyl peak, representing CC stretching vibration.

The XPS characterization was conducted to investigate the elemental composition of the GCD particles as synthesized. A ratio of different core level peaks was obtained from XPS, where the deconvoluted peaks were obtained by oxidation states. The survey spectra of C, N, and O as shown in Fig. 4a. The analysis showed distinct peaks at 285, 400, and 533 eV, indicating the presence of C1s, N1s, and O1s respectively. The percentages for the above- elements were 66.6 %, 1.4 %, and 33.0 % respectively. At 284.0 eV, a strong characterization peak CC/CC bond with high intensity is observed. A low-intensity peak is observed at 285.05 eV which is achieved in the presence of the CN bond. A peak at 286.1 eV and a strong peak at 288.92 eV corresponding to the carbonyl group are also observed (CO and CO) as shown in Fig. 4b. A high-resolution N 1 s spectrum is learned from the two deconvoluted peaks at 400.58 and 401.1 eV, confirming the -NH and CN bond respectively as shown in Fig. 4c. Fig. 4d explains the four deconvoluted peaks positioned at 531.8, 532.4, 532.8, and 534.6 eV represents the core level O 1 s spectrum, revealing the presence of CO, CO, COO-, COC/COH bonds of the developed GCDs. Thus, the XPS spectra determined the different types of binding sites available in the synthesized Delonix regia-derived quantum dots (GCDs) and confirms the presence of amine, hydroxyl, and carboxyl functional groups for GCDs.
The optical properties were studied using the UV–vis absorption spectrum. The purified GCD solution (brown) obtained displayed bright red emissions under UV radiation as shown in Fig. 5a.

Here, the strong absorption peak achieved in the range 282–400 nm can be attributed to π- π * electronic transitions in the carbon domain of GCDs. The weak band obtained in the range of 476–660 nm may be caused due to the overlapping weak n-π * of the carbonyl, hydroxyl, and other functional groups present. A bathochromic shift is noted in the UV spectrum on the addition of Pb2+ ions at 669 nm. Here, the π- π * electronic transitions correspond to the range of 277.5–362 nm whereas, the peaks around 416–669 nm range are explained by the n-π * transition overlapping. Fig. 5b explains the excitation and emission spectra of GCD which are positioned at 660 nm and 676 nm respectively. Fig. 6a explains the PL spectra of excitation-dependent emission spectra which were further investigated for the photophysical properties of the GCDs. As the wavelength was increased, an increase in PL intensity is noticed from 410 nm to 530 nm and then decreased from 540 to 610 nm. The area normalized figure of the same exhibits the redshift behavior of the PL maxima (shown in Fig. 6b). Fig. 6c explains the emission-dependent excitation of synthesized GCD represents two excitation bands that confirm the core and surface state of the synthesized material. The emission-dependent excitation of synthesized GCD ranges from 670 to 700 nm after which are no noticeable changes. Fig. 6d explains the contour mapping of the prepared GCD and it also confirms the GCDs core and surface state. Moreover, the zeta potential distribution value observed was − 29.2 mV. It is suggested that the functionalization of -COOH groups on the surface of the GCD establishes this negative zeta potential value (Fig. 6e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of PL stability of GCD and analysis of Pb2+ ion

ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the fluorescence stability of the GCDs, it was subjected to different pH and ionic conditions. Here, the incubation time suitable for the PL intensity of the GCDs was also studied as in Fig. 7a. The results from PL intensity measurements didn’t show any notable effect for the GCD when irradiated under the UV at 410 nm for 60 mins. Therefore, 60 mins were selected as the optimized time for incubation for the determination of the stability study. The long-term storage stability of the GCDs was studied for 60 days and is explained in Fig. 7b. When the fluorescence intensities were studied over the pH range of 1–14, stability was.

observed in the range of 4–10 as seen in Fig. 7c. and the PL intensities decreased as the solution got too alkaline or too acidic. Upon varying the KCl solution ionic strength from 0 to 2 M, not much difference was observed in the fluorescence intensities of the GCDs as seen in Fig. 7d. All of the above-mentioned results are indications in support of the PL stability of the GCDs.

Studies were conducted with other metal ions as well to determine the selectivity of the synthesized GCDs for lead detection. For this purpose, 400 µl of 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) was taken to which 100 µl GCD was added at normal room temperature. To this, 2 µl of metal ions which are commonly found in water medium along with lead were added and then diluted with 2.5 mL of ethanol to obtain a 16 μM concentration. This solution was then poured into a quartz cuvette and incubated for 2 min. For studying the selectivity, different ions including Pb2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Sn2+, Hg2+, Cr2+, and Fe2+ were employed. The selective PL quenching intensity of GCDs for the specific addition of lead against the different elements is represented in Fig.10(a). This quenching study can be explained by the strong hydrogen bonding interactions occurring between the surface of GCDs (eg: hydroxyl group) and metal ions. [33]. Table 1. explains the comparative study of detection of Pb2+ ion with the reported literature. Further, the interference tests were conducted as shown in ( Fig. 8a). Here, in this GCD containing aqueous solutions, various metal ions (Mn 2+) were added (2 µl) and the quenching effects of Pb2+ and Mn 2+ ions were examined. The reports revealed that the coexistence of different metal ions didn’t have much effect on the quenching effect of Pb2+ ions, thus proving the selectivity of the sensor to Pb2+ ions in mixtures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity determination of GCDs for the selective detection of Pb2+
ce:section.ce:para:
Sensitivity analysis of GCD for Pb2+ was done by recording the PL intensity changes of GCD solution with different concentrations of lead (10–180 μM) which were added. With the increase in the concentration of lead, it was observed that there was a slight bathochromic shift in the emission wavelength from 660 to 669 nm. A decrease in the fluorescence intensity as seen in Fig. 8b clearly explained the quenching effect of lead on GCD. The coordination reaction between Pb2+ ions and phenolic hydroxyl groups as well as the carboxylic groups present on the surface edge of GCD could be responsible for this. The quenching mechanism facilitating the Pb2+ detection is explained as suggested. The high specific selectivity to Pb2+ ion is probably due to the ion-selective environment of the oxygenated functional groups present on the surface of the prepared GCD and the involvement of charge transfer effects. The electron donation happens through a non-radiative type transfer which is better as compared to other metal ions, leading to the formation of a surface complex [28]. The interaction between GCD and the added lead ions may have caused any aggregation of the quantum dots, hence contributing to the quenching effect. The linear range for fluorescence quenching was found to be from 10 μM to 180 μM. The obtained wide linear range was further considered for the calculation of the detection limit with the correlation coefficient (R2 =0.99507) as shown in Fig. 8c. The following equation gives the detection limit,
LOD = 3·3 σ/s

where ‘σ’ represents the sample standard deviation and ‘s′ is the slope of the curve. The detection limit of lead was determined to be 3.3 nM which is good and similar to many results encapsulated in Table 1.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of GCDs can be described by several processes such as (1) Reduction Electron transfer between CQDs and chromophores (2) Induced coordination aggregation (3) FRET (Forster resonance electron transfer). The PL quenching of GCDs by lead is mostly explained using the FRET mechanism. It is known that dipole-dipole associations give rise to FRET wherein the donor fluorophore when undergoing excitation, donates electrons to the acceptor chromophore non-radiatively. In the presence of another dipole having similar energy as compared to the oscillating dipole of the fluorophore, an energy exchange takes place owing to the resonance. The acceptor chromophore used in FRET studies must not be fluorescent. From Fig. 8d it can be seen that lead acts as the non-fluorescent acceptor chromophore and GCDs act as a donor fluorophore. According to FRET, the criteria for the mechanism of quenching obeys the following (i) overlap of the absorption spectra of GCDs + lead and PL emission of GCDs must occur (ii) There must be a reduction in the PL intensity with increasing concentration of lead (iii) GCDs-lead should be in close proximity [35].

Time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) studies were also performed to confirm the quenching mechanism. TCSPC was used to study the fluorescence lifetime of GCDs in the presence and absence of lead (Fig.10(d)). The obtained results of the same were represented in a bi-exponential fit and decay emission intensity was determined. Thus, it was seen that there was no quenching of GCD’s fluorescence lifetime by lead, but the mechanism for quenching was due to the ground state interaction of GCDs with lead. In the presence and absence of lead, the average lifetime was found to be 15.37 and 15.21 ns respectively. This suggests that with the addition of lead, a reduction in the fluorescent lifetime was observed therefore eliminating the IFE mechanism.

From the results, it can be seen that FRET is the major mechanism taking place between the prepared GCD and lead. A non-fluorescent complex is also formed by the adsorption of lead on the GCDs due to electrostatic interactions between the GCD and lead. Thus, the high selectivity of GCD in lead detection can be explained by the FRET from GCD to lead and non-fluorescent complex formation between these.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Pb2+ ion in waste-water samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescent GCDs that were prepared, were employed in the Pb2+ detection of the wastewater samples. The different samples were taken from river water, tap water, and lake water, all of which were located close to different industrial area. Filtration was then carried out with Whatman filter paper. various concentrations of Pb2+ were added to the solution and the lead detection was carried out by using the suggested procedure. The recovery values are in the range of 99–101 % for various water samples and 98–100 % for industrial effluents. The synthesized fluorescing GCD has been successfully used for lead detection in real samples.
Tables 2 and 3.

In this work, a highly water-soluble and fluorescence nature of GCD was prepared from Delonix regia leaves by a simple hydrothermal synthesis set-up. The GCDs prepared displayed an average particle size of 675 ± 2.5 nm, amorphous nature, and contained amine and -OH functional groups. They underwent a test for shelf-life stability and showcased good PL spectra as well as a high quantum yield of 7% in solution. Upon irradiating with a UV source, the aqueous solution containing GCDs showed a bright red fluorescence and the PL studies reveal excellent excitation-dependent fluorescent emission spectra at 676 nm. Here, the GCDs also exhibited good photostability, also retaining their quenching effect throughout a large range of pH and ionic studies, and hence were identified as a potential candidate for sensing applications. A detection limit of 3.3 nM (low value) and a wide linear range of 10–180 µM were achieved for sensing lead in water analysis. Moreover, the quantum dots were studied for their effectiveness in real water samples collected from various water sources near industries, giving a fair recovery rate of 99–101%. Therefore, the authors propose a green, economic and rapid technique for heavy-metal-lead detection in water samples.

Ann Mariella Babu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft; Geethanjali Bijoy: Writing – original draft; Keerthana P: Resources, Formal analysis; Anitha Varghese: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have no competing interests with the work presented in this manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The researchers and authors express their gratitude to the Major research project (MRP DSC-1830), Center for Research, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bangalore, Karnataka, India for providing the required financial support for completing this research work.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lead toxicity in cereals: mechanistic insight into toxicity, mode of action, and management
Front. Plant Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Aslam, A. Aslam, M. Sheraz, B. Ali, Z. Ulhassan, U. Najeeb, W. Zhou, R.A. Gill, Lead Toxicity in Cereals: Mechanistic Insight Into Toxicity, Mode of Action, and Management, Front. Plant Sci. 11 (2021) 2248. https://doi.org/10.3389/FPLS.2020.587785/BIBTEX.

Curr. Opin. Environ. Sci. Heal

Sens. Actuators B Chem.
